data.title: Digital Realty Trust (DLR) Q1 2026 Revenue Sets Benchmark For Data Center REIT Narratives
data.description:
Digital Realty Trust (DLR) has opened 2026 with Q1 revenue of US$1.6b and basic EPS of US$0.49, while trailing twelve month revenue sits at US$6.3b and EPS at US$3.91, setting a clear benchmark for its data center REIT peers this earnings season. Over recent periods the company has seen revenue move from US$1.4b in Q1 2025 to US$1.6b in Q1 2026, with trailing twelve month EPS rising from US$1.74 to US$3.91, giving investors a clean view of how the top line and per share earnings are tracking into the new year. With those figures in hand, the focus now turns to how sustainably margins are being maintained and what that means for the quality of reported profitability.

See our full analysis for Digital Realty Trust.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings print lines up against the most common narratives around Digital Realty Trust, highlighting where the story matches expectations and where it starts to look different.

See what the community is saying about Digital Realty Trust

Advertisement

FFO and margins tell a different story

  • Over the last year, funds from operations reached US$2.4b on US$6.3b of revenue, while net income excluding extras was US$1.3b and the reported 21.2% net margin includes a US$727.2m one off gain.
  • Consensus narrative expects steady demand for AI and cloud driven data center capacity to support adjusted EBITDA and FFO. However, the large one off gain means recent EPS growth of 242.7% and the 21.2% margin do not fully reflect the underlying run rate, so FFO and the five year earnings CAGR of 5.6% give a more grounded check on the bullish story.
    • Supporters of the bullish view point to a record lease backlog and new developments in markets like Charlotte and Atlanta, but the US$727.2m one off in the trailing figures shows a big part of the recent profit jump is not from recurring operations.
    • The forecast for earnings to decline about 1.8% per year over the next three years contrasts with the bullish focus on future FFO growth. This puts more weight on how efficiently that US$2.4b of FFO turns into sustainable cash returns.
On top of that FFO backdrop, bulls are effectively betting that strong leasing demand and new AI focused capacity will matter more than the profit drag from lower forecast margins and the absence of another US$727.2m one off in future results. You can see this unpacked in much more detail in the current bull case for DLR 🐂 Digital Realty Trust Bull Case.

Rich P/E against peers despite 6.2% DCF gap

  • Digital Realty trades on a trailing P/E of 52.2x versus a North American Specialized REITs average of 28.7x and a peer average of 40.5x, even though the US$200 share price sits about 6.2% below the DCF fair value of roughly US$213.25.
  • Bears argue that paying a premium multiple is hard to justify when forecasts point to earnings declining about 1.8% per year over the next three years and revenue growth of roughly 9% a year, which is below an 11% US market revenue growth figure. On this view, the valuation premium rests on slower earnings and margin compression from around 20.9% to 11.0%.
    • Critics also highlight that to align with a separate analyst price target of US$210.86 using earnings of US$897.5m by 2029, the implied P/E would need to more or less double from around 53.9x today to 102.4x, which is far above a referenced 30.5x industry P/E.
    • Set against that, the DCF fair value of US$213.25 and a 2.44% dividend yield mean some investors may see compensation for the premium P/E as long as cash flows and payouts stay on track with forecasts.
Skeptics who focus on the 52.2x P/E and the forecasted earnings dip can compare that caution against the detailed bear arguments around supply risk, interest costs and competition in the dedicated bear case for DLR 🐻 Digital Realty Trust Bear Case.

Debt costs and interest coverage under the microscope

  • Financial analysis flags that interest payments are not well covered by earnings, even though trailing twelve month net income excluding extra items is US$1.3b and FFO is US$2.4b, so servicing debt is a key pressure point alongside growth forecasts.
  • Consensus style commentary sees rapid expansion in US data center markets and up to US$10.0b of planned hyperscale investments as an opportunity to widen revenue to around US$8.2b by 2029. At the same time, higher financing needs, the forecast margin shift from about 20.9% to 11.0% and already weak interest coverage mean the balance between growth projects and borrowing costs is central to the risk side of the thesis.
    • Bears warn that if capital markets become more expensive or harder to access, the combination of rising debt costs and shrinking profit margins could make that interest coverage weakness more visible in future net income figures.
    • Supporters of the expansion plan point to renewable powered facilities and fixed escalators on renewal leases as potential offsets, but the flagged interest coverage issue keeps leverage and funding terms front and center for anyone tracking this REIT.

Next Steps

data.description:

Approach 1: Labrador Iron Ore Royalty Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to a single present value figure.

For Labrador Iron Ore Royalty, the model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is CA$97.07 million, and Simply Wall St has extended analyst style projections out over the next decade. For example, free cash flow is projected at CA$116 million in 2027, with further years based on extrapolated estimates that gradually level off over time.

When all those projected cash flows are discounted back to today, the DCF model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of CA$24.11 per share. Compared with the recent share price of about CA$28.36, this implies the stock is around 17.6% overvalued according to this method.

Based on this model, the current price appears to be at a premium to the DCF estimate for Labrador Iron Ore Royalty.

Result: OVERVALUED

Approach 2: Labrador Iron Ore Royalty Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Labrador Iron Ore Royalty, the P/E ratio is a useful way to think about what you are paying for each dollar of earnings. Investors typically expect higher P/E ratios where earnings growth prospects appear stronger or risks seem lower, and lower P/E ratios where growth looks more modest or risks appear higher.

Labrador Iron Ore Royalty is currently trading on a P/E of 18.0x. That sits close to the Metals and Mining industry average P/E of 18.6x, while the broader peer group used here trades on a much higher average of 47.3x. On the surface, that peer gap might suggest the stock is priced more conservatively than some comparables.

Simply Wall St also provides a “Fair Ratio” of 14.0x, which reflects the P/E that might be expected given factors such as earnings growth characteristics, profit margin profile, industry, market cap and key risks. This Fair Ratio can be more informative than a simple peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for those fundamentals rather than assuming all companies deserve similar multiples. Since the current 18.0x P/E stands above the 14.0x Fair Ratio, the shares look expensive on this measure.

data.title: Hartford Insurance Group (HIG) Margin Improvement To 14% Tests Cautious Earnings Narratives
data.description:
Hartford Insurance Group reported Q1 2026 revenue of US$7.2b and basic EPS of US$3.08, with trailing twelve month revenue at US$28.8b and EPS at US$14.44. Over the past year, revenue has moved from US$26.9b to US$28.8b, while EPS has gone from US$10.20 to US$14.44 on a trailing basis. This gives you a clearer view of how the top and bottom lines have tracked together. Margins have shifted higher over the last twelve months, making this earnings release a key reference point for assessing how sustainably the business is converting revenue into profit.

See our full analysis for Hartford Insurance Group.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how these results line up with the prevailing stories about Hartford Insurance Group, and where the recent performance challenges or supports those narratives.

See what the community is saying about Hartford Insurance Group

Advertisement

Margins Step Up To 14% On Trailing Basis

  • Over the last 12 months, net profit margin sits at 14% on US$28.8b of revenue and US$4.0b of net income, compared with 11% margin a year earlier.
  • Analysts' consensus view expects margins to ease slightly over time, and that sits in contrast with the recent move from 11% to 14%, which:
    • Supports the bullish idea that disciplined underwriting and E&S growth can lift profitability when combined ratios stay in the high 80s, as seen with the 88.3% trailing ratio reported for 2025.
    • Challenges bearish worries that higher catastrophe losses will immediately compress profitability, because the trailing margin improvement has occurred despite those cited events.

Earnings Growth Vs Forecast 0.4% Decline

  • Trailing earnings grew 36.2% year over year with a 5 year earnings CAGR of 14.6%, while consensus forecasts call for earnings to slip about 0.4% per year over the next three years.
  • Bears focus on that 0.4% expected earnings decline, and the tension with the recent 36.2% growth matters because:
    • It suggests the recent pace of EPS growth to US$14.44 on a trailing basis may not be repeated if the forecast moderation in margins from 13.4% to 12.6% plays out.
    • It also means investors need to weigh the 5 year 14.6% earnings growth record against the risk that earnings settle closer to the forecast path rather than the latest 12 month trend.

On a quarter to quarter view, Q1 2026 EPS of US$3.08 compares with US$4.05 in Q4 2025, so it helps to see how investors framing the longer term bull case are thinking about that.

Bulls argue that the story is less about one quarter and more about whether digital investments and E&S expansion can support future EPS around the current trailing US$14.44 level and beyond, which:

  • Lines up with the consensus assumption that earnings could reach about US$4.1b and EPS of US$16.15 by around April 2029, compared with about US$3.8b of earnings today.
  • Relies on continued benefits from technology and data integration and from growth in small business and international markets, all of which are expected to support revenue growth of 4.2% per year even as margins are modeled to ease slightly.

Skeptical investors who want to see how that optimistic case is built out in more detail can review the full bull argument in the 🐂 Hartford Insurance Group Bull Case

  • Consensus narrative flags both potential rewards and risks around that gap, because:
    • The apparent discount to the US$329.03 DCF fair value and the lower P/E than peers fit with the idea that the market is not fully reflecting the recent 36.2% earnings growth and 14% margin.
    • The same data set also includes the forecast 0.4% annual earnings decline and slower 3.9% revenue growth versus an 11% market figure, which helps explain why some investors may hesitate to treat the valuation gap as a simple mispricing.
